notes.note: The "Upper Sea" (mare Superum) is the Adriatic, on Italy's eastern coast, as opposed to the "Lower Sea" (the Tyrrhenian) on the west. It was the route Pompey took from Brundisium to Greece, and the question is whether Cicero should follow him across it.

Demetrius of Magnesia was a Greek scholar and friend of Atticus; his treatise On Concord (Peri homonoias) suited Cicero's anxious meditation on whether peace between Caesar and Pompey was still possible. Cicero notes he has now sent the book back, the hope of a peace settlement having collapsed.

notes.anchor: the most ancient and most sacred parent of all, the fatherland
notes.note: Cicero deploys the commonplace that the patria (fatherland) is the citizen's supreme parent, owed a piety greater than that owed to a natural mother or father. The body-politic figure sharpens his horror that Pompey's leaders would starve Italy itself.

Rome depended on imported grain, chiefly from Sicily, Sardinia, and Africa; controlling these "grain-bearing provinces" meant controlling the city's food supply. Pompey's plan to seize them and blockade Italy is what Cicero condemns as a war begun by famine.

notes.anchor: the matter not come to an interregnum
notes.note: An interregnum was the constitutional gap when no consuls held office; patrician senators then served in turn as interrex to hold the consular elections. Caesar wished to avoid this so that consuls could be elected by a praetor under his control, a procedure Cicero insists is unlawful.
The Liberalia was the festival of the god Liber (17 March), the day on which young Roman men customarily assumed the toga virilis. Cicero uses it here simply as a dateline for the letter.
